text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to be talking about primary abandonment of inguinal scrotal hernia sacs.
This is a technique I've been practicing now for about a year and I've been very happy with my preliminary results. This technique has been described now for a few years.
out of the group from Brazil, where larger hernia sacs are no longer attempted to be reduced, but rather abandoned to reduce the potential for injury to the cord elements.
This will not be a full-length case, but rather of video clips sort of focused around the dissection around the deep ring and the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon begins by incising the peritoneum at the deep ring, aiming for a circumferential transection of the hernia neck at this level. The medial portion of the incision may present challenges.
The surgeon successfully transects and dissociates the distal hernia sac from the peritoneum, completing the circumferential transection at the deep ring.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to establish the critical view, aiming to access the parietal compartment during a standard TAPP inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon performs a general dissection towards the pubis, followed by lateral dissection in the visceral compartment.
The surgeon manipulates areolar tissue fibers, positioning them against the lateral sidewall, while isolating the peritoneum. An intentional hole is visible at the level of the deep ring.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the deep ring to gather the cord elements, blood vessels, and areolar tissue against the lateral sidewall, minimizing the risk of injury.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens during the parietalization process, with the medial dissection not shown in this segment.
The surgeon pulls on the obliterated umbilical artery while grabbing the peritoneum to parietalize the cord elements, creating a landing zone for the mesh.
The surgeon completes a thorough dissection, ensuring a clear area is prepared for the placement of an extra-large mesh.
The surgeon tidies up the surgical field, dissecting around the obturator to identify and address a small amount of preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the obturator space.
The surgeon holds the obliterated umbilical artery, using it as a visual marker to confirm adequate parietalization of the vas deferens.
The surgeon incises and cuts through the obliterated umbilical artery, creating space for the mesh to lay flat against the surrounding tissue.
in the canal, we don't worry about it, we don't mess with it, and we don't induce hematomas. The mesh that I used in this case was an extra large mid-weight.

Some may ask why extra large mid-weight rather than heavyweight for an inguinal scrotal, and I really look at the morphology of the defect. This is a relatively small defect with a large penetrating sac.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure, closing the primary transection of the peritoneum. Once closed, the surgeon proceeds to close the fenestration associated with the abandoned sac.
Pretty standard. We won't show the entire closure of the flap, but that's what it looks like. And hopefully this helps you if you're thinking of adopting this approach. Thank you.
